title: Complete Benchmark Evaluation

black_box.description: Attacks crafted against our Supervisor, tested on unseen targets

synthesis: Across all four settings, a coherent narrative emerges. In white-box conditions, SEAR achieves substantial, real damage (60.9% F1 reduction) using a perturbation 12-30x smaller than unconstrained baselines, demonstrating the efficiency-imperceptibility trade-off central to this thesis. In black-box conditions, none of the attacks — including SEAR — transfer reliably across architectures, indicating that white-box vulnerability does not imply broad real-world risk across the forensic tool ecosystem; the one partial exception (SEAR against Mantra-Net) is plausibly explained by shared sensitivity to noise-domain artifacts. The foundation-model-defense setting reveals that ForensicsSAM's built-in adversarial correction mechanism imposes a real accuracy cost on both clean and attacked inputs alike, an accuracy-robustness trade-off rather than a clean win. Most significantly, the retrained-defense setting shows that once a defender knows about SEAR specifically — which is exactly what this thesis's Attribution Module and Spatial-Concentration Detector are designed to reveal — they can adversarially fine-tune to neutralize it almost completely (F1 recovers from 0.0955 to 0.2460, nearly matching the 0.2495 clean baseline) with no cost to clean accuracy, though this defense remains attack-specific and does not generalize to FGSM, BIM, or MIM. Together, these results support a central thesis claim: explainability is not incidental to defense, it is a precondition for building an effective, targeted one.
